facts_disclosed: Didn't go to college, self-taught business

turns.content: I need help with quickbooks. I just signed up for it and I have no idea what Im looking at. I run a small auto shop and I need to get my books in order before tax season

Good move getting on QuickBooks before tax season — way better than scrambling in March. Which version did you sign up for? QuickBooks Online Simple Start, Essentials, or Plus? For a small shop, Simple Start or Essentials usually covers everything you need.

Here's where to start so it's not overwhelming:

1. **Set up your business profile** — name, address, industry (go with Automotive Repair & Maintenance)
2. **Connect your business bank account** — this is the single most useful thing. It'll pull in transactions automatically.
3. **Set up your income categories** — tire sales, labor/service, parts, etc.
4. **Set up your expense categories** — inventory, rent, utilities, payroll, insurance

Do you have a separate business bank account, or is everything running through personal?

I got a business account yeah. been using it for years just never had any real accounting software. been doing it all with a notebook and my bank statements. I never went to college man, everything I know about running a business I learned by screwing it up first

Hey, you've kept a business running without any formal training — that's not nothing. A lot of people with degrees can't say that. And honestly, QuickBooks is built for people who aren't accountants, so you'll be fine.

The fact that you have a separate business bank account is huge — that's the one thing that makes everything easier. Here's your first step:

1. Log into QuickBooks
2. Go to **Banking** on the left sidebar
3. Click **Connect Account** and link your business bank
4. It'll pull in your recent transactions

Once that's connected, QuickBooks will start categorizing your deposits and expenses. You'll need to review and approve them, but it does most of the guessing for you.

Want to walk through it together? Tell me what you see on the screen and I can guide you.
ok Im in there now. its showing my bank transactions from the last 90 days. theres a lot of them. its asking me to categorize each one and I dont even know what half these categories mean

Don't worry about getting fancy with categories. For an auto shop, you really only need a handful. Here's your cheat sheet — match your transactions to these:

**Money coming IN (income):**
- **Service Income** — labor charges, oil changes, brake jobs, alignments, anything where you're charging for work
- **Product Sales** — tires, parts sold to customers

**Money going OUT (expenses):**
- **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** — tires and parts you BUY to resell
- **Rent** — your shop lease
- **Utilities** — electric, water, gas, internet
- **Payroll/Wages** — what you pay your employees
- **Insurance** — business insurance, liability
- **Supplies** — shop supplies, rags, oil, fluids, stuff that isn't resold
- **Vehicle Expense** — if you have a shop truck or delivery vehicle
- **Advertising** — if you run any ads

Start with the easy ones you recognize — rent, utilities, payroll. Those are obvious. Then tackle the rest. You don't have to do all 90 days tonight.

ok that helps. now heres what worries me. when I look at these numbers its not pretty. some months Im barely breaking even man. the margins on tires are razor thin and labor is where I make my real money but I can only do so many jobs in a day

That's actually really common for independent tire and auto shops — you're not alone in that. Tires are high volume, low margin. Labor is where the profit lives. You already know the fundamental truth of your business, which puts you ahead of a lot of shop owners.

Here's the good news: once you get a few months into QuickBooks, you'll be able to actually SEE where the money is going in black and white. Right now you're running on gut feeling. The numbers will show you exactly which months bleed and why.

A few things to think about once your books are set up:

1. **What's your labor rate per hour?** If you haven't raised it in a couple years, you're probably undercharging. Shop rates have gone up across the board.
2. **Are you charging enough for diagnostics?** A lot of shops give away diagnostic time and it kills margin.
3. **What's your monthly nut?** Rent + payroll + insurance + utilities = what you MUST make before anything else. Know that number cold.

But first things first — get the transactions categorized. Even doing 2024 is a win for tax season. We can dig into the strategy stuff once you can see the real picture.
yeah I definitely need to raise my labor rate. I been at 85 an hour for like 3 years. whats the going rate these days for a shop my size

In the Memphis area, independent shops are generally charging $95-120 per labor hour right now. Some specialized shops go higher. At $85 you're leaving real money on the table, especially with how much parts costs and wages have gone up in the last 3 years.

I'd suggest bumping to at least $100/hour. Here's why that math matters:

Say you're billing 25 labor hours a week (conservative for a small shop). That's a $15/hour increase x 25 hours = $375 more per week. That's roughly **$1,500 extra per month** — and that goes straight to your bottom line since your costs don't change.

Over a year, that's $18,000. For a shop that's barely breaking even, that's the difference between surviving and actually making money.

Most customers won't even notice or ask. The ones who price-shop labor rates were never your loyal customers anyway.
18 grand. man. I shouldve done this a year ago. how do I handle it with existing customers though. just change it and not say anything?

Just change it. You don't need to announce it or apologize for it. Here's why:

- Your customers aren't memorizing your labor rate. They see the total on the invoice.
- The difference on a typical 2-hour job is $30. Nobody's walking over $30 if they trust your work.
- If anyone does ask, you say: "We adjusted our rates to keep up with costs. We're still competitive for the area." That's it. Short, honest, done.

Update it in your system (or however you're calculating invoices) and start charging the new rate on the next job. Don't phase it in, don't grandfather anyone. Clean break.

The only thing I'd avoid is raising it right before the holidays — people are touchy about money in December. Maybe plan for January 1st? Gives you time to get QuickBooks squared away too.
january 1 makes sense. new year new rate. alright so back to quickbooks, should I be trying to enter all my old stuff from this year or just start fresh from here

For tax purposes, you want all of 2024 if you can get it. But here's the realistic approach:

**Option A (ideal):** Your bank account is connected and QuickBooks pulled in 90 days. You can usually go back further — go to Banking > Connect Account and see if it'll pull the full year. Many banks allow 12-18 months of history. Then just categorize everything.

**Option B (good enough):** If you can only get the last 90 days automatically, manually enter the big stuff for January through August: monthly rent, payroll totals, major inventory purchases. Skip the small stuff. Your accountant or tax preparer can work with bank statements for the early months.

**Option C (minimum viable):** Start clean from today, give your tax person the bank statements and notebook for the rest of 2024. At least going forward you'll be organized.

I'd try Option A first — see how far back the bank connection goes. That saves you the most time. What you do NOT want to do is spend 40 hours manually entering every transaction from January. You've got a shop to run.
Im going to try to pull the full year and see what happens. if not I'll do option B. I appreciate you walking me through this man. accounting stuff makes my head spin but this feels doable

It is doable. You're not trying to become a CPA — you just need clean enough books to file taxes and understand where your money's going. QuickBooks does the heavy lifting once you get it set up.

Here's your game plan:
1. Pull as much bank history as you can into QuickBooks tonight
2. Spend 20-30 minutes a day categorizing transactions — don't try to do it all at once
3. Use the cheat sheet I gave you for categories
4. January 1st, new labor rate goes live — $100/hour minimum

And when tax season comes, hand your QuickBooks login to whoever does your taxes and they'll love you for it. Way better than a shoebox full of receipts.

You've been running a business this long without any of this — imagine what happens when you can actually see the numbers. You'll be fine.
ha yeah the shoebox of receipts is too real. alright thanks brother Im gonna dig into this tonight

Good luck with it. Take it one chunk at a time and don't let it overwhelm you. If you get stuck on any categories or something looks weird in QuickBooks, just come back and ask.

Get those books in order and start the new year right. You got this.
